title: Chapter 3: Times Square
content: The phone vibrated itself off the nightstand.

Aline heard it hit the floor before she understood she was awake — a sharp crack against the hardwood that cut through a dream she couldn't remember. The vibrating didn't stop. It skittered across the floor like something alive, buzzing against the boards in a sustained, desperate tremor, and when she finally reached down and grabbed it, the screen was so bright with notifications that it turned her dark bedroom pale blue.

Forty-seven missed calls. Two hundred and thirteen text messages. Her thumb hovered over the screen, and the previews alone made her stomach lurch — fragments from numbers she didn't recognize, from Mags (twenty-six times), from an email address that was just a string of numbers, all of them some version of the same question, the same capitalized disbelief, the same what the hell what the hell WHAT THE HELL.
She opened Spotify.

The app loaded, and for a long moment she thought it was broken. A glitch. Some kind of mirroring error where the app had loaded someone else's profile over hers, someone with 2.3 million monthly listeners, someone whose song sat at #1 on the Viral 50 chart like it had always been there. Someone whose face stared back from her own artist page — her face, but photographed from an angle she didn't remember, in light she hadn't stood in.

"The Last Song." Her song. The one she'd recorded into a USB microphone at 2 AM in her underwear, voice cracking on the bridge because she couldn't stop crying long enough to get a clean take. That song. Number one.

A notification from Twitter, and she clicked it: a video. Someone had filmed Times Square at 3:17 AM. The footage was shaky, shot from a cab window, and the audio was distorted by wind and traffic noise, but there was no mistaking what she was seeing. Every digital billboard — every single one — displayed the same thing. Her name. Her waveform. The cover art she'd made herself using a photo of her mother's old piano. The words "THE LAST SONG — ALINE RIVERS" spelled forty feet tall in white light, cycling across Samsung and Coca-Cola and whatever else had been purchased, replaced, overridden, and her voice pouring from speakers meant for jingles and car ads, her voice filling the canyons of Midtown Manhattan at three in the morning like a prayer broadcast to no one and everyone.
She watched the video three times.
The fourth time, she noticed her hands were shaking so badly the phone jittered in her grip like a small frightened animal. The fifth time, she called Mags.
Mags picked up before the first ring finished.

"ALINE." Her voice was a scream, raw and breathless, the sound of someone who had been screaming for hours. "I have been calling you for TWO HOURS. Are you SEEING this? Are you on your phone? Are you—"
"I'm seeing it."
"Are you WATCHING? It's still going. It's still on the billboards. The news is there. There's a helicopter. ALINE. There's a helicopter over Times Square because of your SONG."

Aline was silent. She sat on the edge of her bed in the dark and let Mags scream because she couldn't think of a single thing to say. She hadn't done this. She had sung into a microphone and clicked upload and gone to sleep, and that was a thing that ordinary people did every day, and it did not result in this. This was not marketing. Marketing was a slow climb, a strategy, a campaign with a budget and a timeline and a team. This was architecture. Someone had built the infrastructure to make this happen — had written the code, seized the adspace, overridden the feeds, commandeered every screen in a twelve-block radius — and they had done it between 2 AM and 3 AM on a random Thursday while she slept twelve feet away from her vibrating phone.
"Aline? You still there? You okay? You sound weird."
"I didn't do this, Mags."
A pause. Then, quieter: "I know, babe. I know."
The knock came at 7:14 AM.

Aline almost didn't answer it. She was still sitting on her bed, still holding her phone like a grenade with the pin half-pulled, still scrolling through a timeline that had stopped making sense. But the knock came again — three sharp, measured strikes — and she crossed the apartment in her bare feet and opened the door to find a courier in a black cap, no logo, holding a garment box the color of dried blood. Matte black. No label. No receipt. No return address.
"Rivers?" the courier said, already holding it out.
"Who's it from?"
He shrugged. Already walking away.
She closed the door and set the box on the kitchen counter. The lid came off easily, and inside, folded in tissue paper so thin it felt like breath, was a dress.

The silk caught the light from the window and swallowed it. Midnight blue — not navy, not indigo, but the precise color of the sky in the minutes before it surrendered to black — and it moved like water when she lifted it from the box, the fabric sliding over her fingers with a whisper so soft it was nearly a sound she felt rather than heard. Floor-length. Sleeveless. A neckline that followed her collarbones like a question. And at the throat, pinned to the fabric with the precision of a surgeon, a brooch: a black butterfly, wings spread, no larger than her thumbnail. Onyx body. Obsidian wings. The metal was cold when she touched it, and heavy — denser than it looked, as though the thing carried real weight, real substance, instead of the hollow prettiness of costume jewelry.

She knew what this dress would cost. She'd worked in a boutique in the Village for two years, learning to read price tags the way other people read horoscopes — for predictions about a future they couldn't afford. This silk was hand-dyed. The seams were invisible. The brooch alone was worth more than every possession she'd ever owned, combined, sold on a good day.
No note. No card. Nothing but the dress and the butterfly and the silence of her kitchen at seven in the morning.
She knew who it was from.

The contradiction landed in her chest like a stone dropped into still water: the dress was beautiful. The butterfly was a cage. Both things were true, and neither canceled the other, and she was standing in her apartment holding a garment that cost more than the building she stood in, and she could not make herself put it down.
Her phone rang. Unknown number.
She answered.
"Do you like it?"
Cass.

His voice was warm — not smooth, not rehearsed, but genuinely warm, carrying something that sounded terrifyingly like pride. Like a father watching his child ride a bicycle for the first time. Like a sculptor stepping back from marble. The intimacy of it was obscene. He spoke as if they'd been having a conversation for years, as if this were simply the latest sentence in a dialogue she'd been part of all along.
"What did you do?" Her voice came out steady. She didn't know how.
"I made sure the world could hear what I've been hearing for six months."
The word hit her before the meaning did. *Six.* She replayed it.
"Six months?"

A pause. She could hear him breathing — slow, measured, deliberate. She could almost hear him choosing his words, sorting through options the way a jeweler sorts through stones, selecting the one that would catch the light at exactly the angle he intended.
"I've been listening for a long time, Aline. You just didn't know it."

The implications didn't arrive one at a time. They crashed over her in a single wave, cold and total, pulling the floor out from under everything she thought she understood about the last six months of her life. A metallic taste flooded the back of her throat — adrenaline or fear or both, she couldn't tell the difference anymore. Six months. Before the offer. Before last night's phone call. Before she ever knew he existed. He had been listening while she sang in the shower. While she talked to Mags on speakerphone. While she made coffee and hummed old songs and sat at her keyboard and played the same four chords over and over, thinking no one would ever hear.
She sat down on the bed. The phone pressed against her ear felt like a hand.
"How?" she whispered.
"Does it matter?"

Before she could answer, her phone buzzed — an incoming call, cutting through. She pulled it away from her ear and saw the name: Gerald Platt — Landlord. She hadn't spoken to Platt in four months, not since the last rent increase, not since he'd stood in this same doorway with his clipboard and his watery eyes and told her the building was going condo and she should start looking.
She switched over.
"Ms. Rivers." Platt's voice was different. Unctuous. Almost friendly. "I wanted to let you know personally — your rent has been paid. In full. For the next twelve months."
Her mouth opened. Nothing came out.
"The entire year," he repeated, as though she might not have heard. "We received the payment early this morning. Wire transfer. Very generous. You won't need to worry about—"
She hung up. Switched back.
He was still there. Waiting.
"You paid my rent."
"I take care of what's mine."

The words landed in her sternum like a fist. Something hot and sharp and furious rose through her chest, burning through the gratitude she hadn't even realized she was beginning to feel, incinerating it.
"I'm not yours."
Silence.
Not the silence of being at a loss. The silence of being perfectly still. Of watching. Of waiting with the patience of something that had never once doubted the answer.
Then, soft: "Not yet."
She hung up.

The apartment was quiet. The morning light had shifted while she'd been on the phone, and now it fell across the bed in a long golden bar, illuminating the dress where she'd laid it on the sheets. Midnight blue against white. The butterfly brooch caught the light and threw it back in a thin black sparkle, and for a moment the thing seemed to move, seemed to flex its wings against the silk like it was waking up.

She should take it off. The brooch. The dress. She should strip them both and stuff them back in the box and leave the box in the hallway for someone else to find. She should call the police. She should call Mags back and tell her everything — not just the billboards, not just the overnight fame, but the call, the dress, the six months, the rent, the way his voice had said *not yet* like it was a promise and a threat and a prayer all folded into two syllables.
She didn't.

She picked up the dress and stepped into it. The silk slid up her legs and over her hips and settled against her skin like it had been made for her — because of course it had, because he'd been watching, because he'd been measuring her somehow from a distance she couldn't imagine, and even knowing that, even feeling the violation of it settling against her like a second skin, she could not deny what her body already knew: the dress fit. The silk was cool and fluid against her and it moved when she moved and it smelled faintly of something she couldn't name — cedar, maybe, or sandalwood, or the ghost of a room she'd never been in.

She pinned the butterfly to her collar. The metal was still cold. The weight of it surprised her again — that a thing so small could carry so much. It sat against the hollow of her throat, and when she breathed, she felt it rise and fall.
She crossed the apartment to the bathroom and looked in the mirror.
A stranger stared back.

The woman in the mirror was beautiful. Adorned. She wore midnight blue and onyx and the kind of composure that comes from being seen — truly seen — for the first time. But the woman in the mirror was also owned. Every thread of the dress, every facet of the brooch, every pixel on every billboard, every dollar of paid rent — they all led back to one point, one person, one voice in the dark that said *mine* like it was the most natural word in his mouth.

But *visible*. For the first time in twenty-six years of being overlooked and underheard and just slightly out of focus in every room she'd ever entered, the woman in the mirror was undeniably, unmistakably, violently visible.
She leaned closer to her reflection. The butterfly pulsed against her throat.
"Who the hell are you?"

Somewhere beneath the city, in a room with no windows, Cass watched the butterfly's GPS signal pulse steady on his screen. A small green dot, rhythmical, alive, marking her position in the apartment exactly where he'd last calculated she would be. The monitor's blue light washed over his face — sharp jaw, darker eyes, the hollowed cheeks of a man who had not slept and did not need to. Six other screens surrounded him. Code cascading. Audio waveforms from her apartment, faint with the sound of her breathing. The Times Square feed, still cycling her name in white light. A camera view from the hallway outside her door, empty now, the courier long gone.
He reached out and touched the monitor where her face was — the reflection, caught by the bathroom camera, the moment her eyes met her own. His fingertips pressed against the cold glass.
"Yours," he said. "I'm yours."
